intuition: To invert a binary tree, swap the left and right children of every node. This can be done recursively — invert the children first, then swap them. Or swap first, then recurse — both work.
approach: Use DFS recursion. Base case: if the node is null, return null. Otherwise, recursively invert left and right subtrees, then swap the node's left and right children. Return the node.

complexity.space: O(h) — recursion stack depth equals tree height

hints: What does 'invert' mean at a single node? Just swap left and right.
If you invert every node's children, the whole tree is inverted.
Think recursively: invert the subtrees, then swap them at the current node.
commonMistakes: Only swapping the root's children without recursing — the subtrees stay un-inverted.
Forgetting the null base case — leads to null pointer errors.
author: chron303
